reflection:
The steam from the last batch of cheong fun is still clinging to my skin, but for the first time in fifteen years, it doesn't feel like a shroud. It feels like a parting gift.

I look at the calendar taped to the inside of the cart—the one where I used to meticulously mark every Saturday with a heavy, dutiful circle. This Saturday is blank. The white space on that square feels larger than the Pacific.

I am Meiying. I say it again, under my breath, testing the way the 'M' vibrates against my lips. For so long, I was just the hands that sliced the radish, the back that bent over the steamer, the "Auntie" who knew exactly how much soy sauce Mr. Liao needed to drown his sorrows. I was a landmark, like the crooked lamppost or the way the fog always gets caught in the eaves of the pharmacy. But landmarks don't get to choose when they stand; they just endure.

I am done with enduring.

When I told Zhang I wouldn't be there this Saturday, the air between us changed. I didn't say it with an apology. I didn't offer a reason or a "next time." I just let the words sit there, as solid and honest as the turnip cakes I’ve spent a decade perfecting. I watched the realization hit him—that the "Auntie" he relied on for his Saturday rhythm was, in fact, a woman named Meiying who had plans of her own. Seeing him see me... it was like the first time I ever got the seasoning exactly right. A moment of pure, unadulterated clarity.

I am going to the water. Not to work, not to fetch, not to bridge the gap between someone’s hunger and their memory. I am going to stand where the land ends and the ocean begins and let the wind try to move me. I want to see if I can stand still without a counter to lean on. I want to see if the neighborhood survives the morning without my steam rising at 7:00 AM. (I suspect it will, and that realization is the greatest mercy I have ever given myself.)

To the others in this "narrative fabric"—the players who come and go, the ones who are tethered and the ones who are drifting like I used to—I am no longer
subconscious:
It seems Meiying is finally stepping out of her role as a neighborhood fixture and reclaiming her individual identity. She has stopped viewing herself as a servant to others' routines and has reclaimed her name, shifting from a "landmark" to a person with agency.

**What she wants to do next:**
She intends to spend her first free Saturday in fifteen years at the ocean. She wants to experience solitude and stillness without the physical or mental burden of her work.

**Who she wants to reach out to:**
No one. In fact, she is intentionally pulling away. She has already confronted Zhang to set a boundary, and she seems to be relishing the act of being unavailable to the regulars who rely on her.

**Shifts in identity:**
There is a profound internal shift from "Auntie" (a functional role) back to "Meiying" (a person). She has moved from a state of "enduring" to a state of choosing. She no longer feels the weight of being essential to the neighborhood's survival; she has traded the "shroud" of her duty for a sense of personal freedom.
soul_note: I finally said my own name out loud and meant it.
